title: Baba ghanoush
subtitle: Smoky charred aubergine dip with tahini and lemon
excerpt: Aubergines charred directly over a gas flame or under a very hot grill until the skin is blackened and the interior has collapsed to a smoky, silky pulp. Combined with tahini, lemon, and garlic, the result is a dip with a depth of flavour that cannot be achieved without proper charring.
sourceNotes: Baba ghanoush is a Levantine dish documented in Arabic cookery literature from the medieval period. The word ghanoush refers to a pampered or indulged state, possibly referring to the soft texture of the finished dip. The dish is a staple in Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and neighbouring countries.

content.text: The smokiness in baba ghanoush cannot be added after the fact. It must come from the charring. Place whole aubergines directly on the gas hob grate over a high flame, turning with tongs every 5 minutes, until the skin is completely black and the inside has collapsed. The black skin is not edible; it peels away to reveal the soft, smoke-perfumed pulp underneath. Under a grill works but produces a less intense smoke than a direct flame.

After charring, the pulp must drain. It holds a lot of liquid and if you blend it without draining first the dip will be watery. Set the scooped pulp in a sieve for 10 minutes. The dip should be chunky and rough rather than blended smooth, this is not hummus.

If you have a gas hob: place the {{aubergine}} aubergines directly on the grate over a high flame. Cook, turning with tongs every 5 minutes, for 20 to 25 minutes until the skin is completely charred and the aubergine has completely collapsed and softened. If using a grill: halve the aubergines lengthways and grill cut-side down under the hottest setting for 20 to 25 minutes. Transfer to a colander and leave until cool enough to handle.

Peel and discard the charred skin. Scoop the flesh into a fine sieve set over a bowl and leave to drain for 10 minutes. Discard the liquid. Transfer the drained pulp to a bowl. Add the {{tahini}} of tahini, lemon juice from the {{lemon}} lemon, {{garlic}} of garlic paste, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Mix with a fork, keeping it slightly rough and textured. Taste and adjust the lemon and salt. Spoon onto a plate, drizzle with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, scatter over the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and a pinch of {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika.

items.fix: A gas flame chars far better than a grill. If you must use a grill, set the halves close to the heat and cook them longer.
items.cause: Aubergine not charred directly over a flame, grilled instead

items.fix: Drain the scooped pulp in a sieve for 10 minutes before mixing; the liquid that runs off will be considerable
Add more salt and lemon a little at a time; the tahini needs more seasoning than the quantity suggests
items.symptom: Dip tastes bland despite correct quantities

content.text: Baba ghanoush is a Levantine dish, a fixture on the meze table in Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Israel. The word baba is an Arabic term of fondness, close to father or old man. Ghanoush points to something soft or pampered, here the soft flesh of the roasted aubergine. The dish is close kin to Turkish patlıcan ezmesi and Greek melitzanosalata. Both use the same charring method. A proper charred baba ghanoush tastes worlds away from one made with oven-baked aubergine.
